question: A citizen buys a fresh stamp from the post office. Unknown to the citizen, the postal worker illegally sold them a recycled stamp. The citizen uses it. Is the citizen guilty?
answer: No, the statute requires the person to act "fraudulently" and to use a stamp "which he knows to have been before used." An innocent purchaser lacks this knowledge and intent. [Source: Section 184, BNS 2023]

section_title: Using Government stamp known to have been before used
question: What is the consequence of having separate sections for "removing" (Sec 183) and "using" (Sec 184) recycled stamps?
answer: The consequence is that the law can target both ends of the fraud: the "scavenger" who prepares the fake stamps (punished more harshly with 3 years) and the "customer" who actually uses them on documents (punished with 2 years). [Source: Section 184, BNS 2023]

section_title: Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used
question: What is the penalty for washing or erasing the cancellation mark off a used stamp so it looks brand new?
answer: Erasing a cancellation mark from a used government stamp, or possessing/selling such an altered stamp, with intent to cause loss to the government, is punishable by up to three years in prison, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 185, BNS 2023]

section_title: Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used
question: How does Section 185 of BNS 2023 criminalize the alteration of used stamps?
answer: Section 185 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 3 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both, on anyone who fraudulently erases a usage mark from a government stamp, or knowingly possesses, sells, or disposes of a stamp with an erased mark or a known used stamp. [Source: Section 185, BNS 2023]

section_title: Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used
question: A fraudster uses chemical solvents to wash the black cancellation ink off used revenue stamps, making them look mint, and then sells them online as new. What is the maximum sentence?
answer: Because he fraudulently erased a mark denoting the stamp had been used, and sold stamps knowing they had been used, he is punishable under Section 185 with imprisonment up to three years. [Source: Section 185, BNS 2023]

section_title: Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used
question: What three specific distinct activities involving marked stamps are criminalized under this single section?
answer: The three activities are: 1) erasing or removing the usage mark, 2) knowingly possessing, selling, or disposing of a stamp with an erased mark, and 3) selling or disposing of any stamp known to have been used. [Source: Section 185, BNS 2023]

section_title: Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used
question: If a stamp collector uses a gentle solvent to clean a dirty (but unused) 100-year-old stamp to improve its appearance for their private album, is this a crime?
answer: No, because the collector did not erase a "mark... denoting that the same has been used" and did not act "fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to Government," this preservation act is not an offence. [Source: Section 185, BNS 2023]

section_title: Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used
question: What is the consequence of making it a 3-year felony just to *possess* a stamp with an erased cancellation mark?
answer: The consequence is that it severely disrupts the black market for recycled stamps, allowing police to arrest counterfeiters and middlemen simply for holding the tampered inventory, even if they are never caught in the act of erasing the marks or selling them. [Source: Section 185, BNS 2023]

section_title: Prohibition of fictitious stamps
question: What is the penalty for making or mailing a fake postage stamp?
answer: Making, possessing, or using fictitious stamps for postal purposes is punishable by a fine of up to 200 rupees, and the fake stamps/materials will be seized. [Source: Section 186, BNS 2023]

section_title: Prohibition of fictitious stamps
question: How does Section 186 of BNS 2023 penalize the use of fictitious postal stamps?
answer: Section 186 of BNS 2023 imposes a fine up to 200 rupees on anyone who makes, knowingly uses for postal purposes, possesses, or creates a die/instrument for making fictitious stamps. [Source: Section 186, BNS 2023]

section_title: Prohibition of fictitious stamps
question: A hobbyist creates highly realistic international postage stamps using a high-end printer, but just keeps them in a drawer without ever mailing a letter. Can they be fined?
answer: Yes, under sub-section (1)(c), anyone who "has in his possession, without lawful excuse, any fictitious stamp" is punishable by a fine of up to two hundred rupees. [Source: Section 186, BNS 2023]

section_title: Prohibition of fictitious stamps
question: What happens to the fake stamps or printing equipment found in a person's possession upon conviction under this section?
answer: Sub-section (2) mandates that any such stamp, die, plate, instrument or materials in the possession of any person for making any fictitious stamp "shall be seized and, if seized shall be forfeited." [Source: Section 186, BNS 2023]

section_title: Prohibition of fictitious stamps
question: Does the definition of "fictitious stamp" apply to fake stamps of foreign nations, or only Indian stamps?
answer: It applies to both; under sub-section (3), "fictitious stamp" means any stamp falsely purporting to be a postage stamp used by the Government of India or by "the Government of any foreign country." [Source: Section 186, BNS 2023]

section_title: Prohibition of fictitious stamps
question: What is the consequence of treating the counterfeiting of *postage* stamps as a minor fine offence (200 rupees), while counterfeiting *revenue* stamps carries up to 10 years in prison (Section 181)?
answer: The consequence is that the law distinguishes between minor postal fraud (stealing a few rupees of mail delivery service) and serious revenue fraud (evading massive taxes or legal fees on documents), punishing the latter much more severely. [Source: Section 186, BNS 2023]

section_title: Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition from that fixed by law
question: What is the penalty for a government mint employee who secretly shorts the amount of silver going into new coins?
answer: A mint employee who intentionally alters the legal weight or composition of coins being issued is punishable by up to seven years in prison and a fine. [Source: Section 187, BNS 2023]

section_title: Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition from that fixed by law
question: How does Section 187 of BNS 2023 penalize insider tampering at government mints?
answer: Section 187 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine on any employee of a lawfully established mint in India who intentionally acts or omits an act to cause a coin to have a different weight or composition than fixed by law. [Source: Section 187, BNS 2023]

section_title: Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition from that fixed by law
question: A quality-control inspector at a government mint notices a machine is producing coins with 10% less copper than required. To help the factory manager meet quotas and save money, the inspector intentionally approves the batch. What is the inspector's crime?
answer: Because he omitted what he was legally bound to do with the intention of causing coins issued from the mint to be of a different composition, he is punishable under Section 187 with imprisonment up to seven years. [Source: Section 187, BNS 2023]

section_title: Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition from that fixed by law
question: What specific employment status is required for an offender to be charged under this section?
answer: The offender must be a "Person employed in mint lawfully established in India." [Source: Section 187, BNS 2023]

section_title: Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition from that fixed by law
question: A maintenance worker accidentally bumps a dial on a smelting machine, causing the next batch of coins to be slightly overweight. Is the worker guilty under Section 187?
answer: No, the offence requires the person to act or omit an act "with the intention of causing any coin... to be of a different weight or composition." Accidental errors lack this intent. [Source: Section 187, BNS 2023]

section_title: Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition from that fixed by law
question: What is the legal consequence of creating a specialized offence for mint employees?
answer: The consequence is that the law recognizes the unique systemic threat posed by insider corruption at a mint, where even slight, intentional alterations to alloy mixtures can massively devalue the national currency and erode public trust. [Source: Section 187, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint
question: Is it a crime for a mint worker to take a coin-stamping tool home?
answer: Yes, unlawfully taking a coining tool or instrument out of a lawfully established mint is punishable by up to seven years in prison and a fine. [Source: Section 188, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint
question: How does Section 188 of BNS 2023 penalize the theft of mint equipment?
answer: Section 188 of BNS 2023 prescribes up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine for anyone who, without lawful authority, takes any coining tool or instrument out of a lawfully established mint in India. [Source: Section 188, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint
question: A security guard at a government mint finds an old, retired coin die in the trash. He takes it home to keep as a souvenir. What offence has he committed?
answer: Because he took a coining instrument out of the mint without lawful authority, he is punishable under Section 188 with imprisonment up to seven years. [Source: Section 188, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint
question: Does the prosecution need to prove the person intended to use the stolen instrument to make fake coins?
answer: No, the statute only requires proving that the person "without lawful authority, takes out of any mint... any coining tool or instrument." The mere unauthorized removal completes the offence. [Source: Section 188, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint
question: If the Mint Director signs an authorization form for an engineer to take a broken coining press to an external repair shop, is the engineer guilty of taking an instrument from the mint?
answer: No, because the engineer removed the instrument with "lawful authority," the act does not violate Section 188. [Source: Section 188, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint
question: What is the systemic consequence of harshly punishing the unauthorized removal of mint tools, regardless of intent?
answer: The consequence is that the State severely restricts access to the highly specialized tools needed for perfect counterfeiting, ensuring that genuine mint equipment cannot easily fall into the hands of criminal organizations. [Source: Section 188, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawful assembly
question: How many people does it take to form a legally "unlawful assembly," and what must they be doing?
answer: An unlawful assembly requires five or more people who share a common criminal object, such as using force against the government, resisting the law, committing mischief, or forcing someone to do something illegally. [Source: Section 189, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawful assembly
question: How does Section 189 of BNS 2023 define and penalize an unlawful assembly?
answer: Section 189 of BNS 2023 defines an unlawful assembly as 5 or more persons with a common object to overawe the government, resist law, commit offences, or use criminal force to take property or compel actions, punishing members with up to 6 months imprisonment (or 2 years if armed or refusing to disperse). [Source: Section 189, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawful assembly
question: A mob of six people surrounds a farmer, threatening to beat him up unless he signs over the deed to his land. What specific legal definition covers this mob?
answer: Because they are an assembly of five or more persons with the common object of using criminal force to compel a person to do what he is not legally bound to do (sign the deed), they constitute an "unlawful assembly" under sub-section (1)(e). [Source: Section 189, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawful assembly
question: If an assembly starts peacefully to protest a law, but later the crowd decides to start burning police cars, does it become an unlawful assembly?
answer: Yes, under the Explanation to sub-section (1), "An assembly which was not unlawful when it assembled, may subsequently become an unlawful assembly." [Source: Section 189, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawful assembly
question: If four people gather with the common object of committing a criminal trespass, are they an "unlawful assembly"?
answer: No, sub-section (1) explicitly states that an assembly must consist of "five or more persons" to be designated an unlawful assembly. They would just be co-conspirators. [Source: Section 189, BNS 2023]

section_title: Unlawful assembly
question: What is the legal consequence of sub-section (6) regarding people who hire others to join a riot?
answer: The consequence is that the financiers and organizers who hire goons to form an unlawful assembly are punished exactly as if they were physically present in the mob themselves, and are liable for any crimes the hired goons commit. [Source: Section 189, BNS 2023]

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
question: If I am part of a riot and someone else in the crowd throws a brick that hurts a police officer, can I be charged for the injury?
answer: Yes, every member of an unlawful assembly is guilty of any offence committed by another member if the act is done to achieve the group's common object or was known to be a likely outcome. [Source: Section 190, BNS 2023]

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
question: How does Section 190 of BNS 2023 establish collective liability for unlawful assemblies?
answer: Section 190 of BNS 2023 states that if an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object, or is known to be likely committed, every person who is a member of the assembly at that time is guilty of that offence. [Source: Section 190, BNS 2023]

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
question: Fifty people form an unlawful assembly to violently burn down a government building. One member of the crowd throws a firebomb, committing arson. Can a person standing in the back of the crowd who threw nothing be charged with arson?
answer: Yes, because the arson was committed in prosecution of the common object (burning the building), every person who was a member of the assembly at the time is guilty of that offence under Section 190. [Source: Section 190, BNS 2023]

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
question: What are the two alternate conditions under which collective liability attaches to an offence committed by a single member?
answer: The offence must either be 1) committed "in prosecution of the common object" or 2) "such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object." [Source: Section 190, BNS 2023]

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
question: If a group forms an unlawful assembly to peacefully but illegally block a road, and suddenly one member pulls out a gun and shoots his personal rival who happened to be driving by, is the rest of the crowd guilty of murder?
answer: Unlikely. The murder was a personal vendetta, not done "in prosecution of the common object" (blocking the road), nor was it "likely to be committed" in the course of a sit-in. Therefore, the rest of the assembly is not collectively liable. [Source: Section 190, BNS 2023]

section_title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
question: What is the systemic consequence of this strict collective liability principle in riot situations?
answer: The consequence is that it severely deters individuals from participating in violent mobs, as the law holds "bystander" rioters legally responsible for the most extreme crimes committed by the most violent members of their group. [Source: Section 190, BNS 2023]

section_title: Rioting
question: How does the law define a "riot"?
answer: A riot occurs when an unlawful assembly, or any member of it, uses force or violence in prosecution of their common object. [Source: Section 191, BNS 2023]

section_title: Rioting
question: How does Section 191 of BNS 2023 define and punish rioting?
answer: Section 191 of BNS 2023 states that whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly in prosecution of its common object, every member is guilty of rioting, punishable by up to 2 years imprisonment (or 5 years if armed with a deadly weapon), or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 191, BNS 2023]

section_title: Rioting
question: During an unlawful assembly to illegally block a factory, a few members at the front start smashing the factory windows with rocks while the rest just chant. Who is guilty of rioting?
answer: Under sub-section (1), because violence was used by a member of the unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object, *every* member of the assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting. [Source: Section 191, BNS 2023]

section_title: Rioting
question: What specific aggravating factor increases the maximum punishment for rioting from two years to five years under this section?
answer: The maximum punishment increases to five years if the person is "armed with a deadly weapon or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death." [Source: Section 191, BNS 2023]

section_title: Rioting
question: If a group of four people get into a violent fistfight outside a bar to settle a personal score, is it legally a "riot"?
answer: No, because a riot requires the use of force by an "unlawful assembly" (which requires five or more people with a specific common object), four people fighting is an affray or assault, not a riot. [Source: Section 191, BNS 2023]

section_title: Rioting
question: What is the legal consequence of distinguishing "unlawful assembly" (Section 189) from "rioting" (Section 191)?
answer: The consequence is that the law can punish the mere formation of a dangerous mob (unlawful assembly) before any harm is done, but escalates the penalty significantly (rioting) the moment the mob crosses the line into actual physical force or violence. [Source: Section 191, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot-if rioting be committed; if not
question: What is the penalty for intentionally provoking a crowd to start a riot?
answer: Malignantly or wantonly doing an illegal act to provoke a riot is punishable by up to one year in prison if a riot actually occurs, and up to six months if it doesn't. [Source: Section 192, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot-if rioting be committed; if not
question: How does Section 192 of BNS 2023 punish the act of provoking a riot?
answer: Section 192 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 1 year imprisonment if a riot is committed, or up to 6 months if not committed, on anyone who malignantly or wantonly does an illegal act intending or knowing it will provoke a riot. [Source: Section 192, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot-if rioting be committed; if not
question: An agitator stands on a car and illegally burns a religious text, shouting insults to intentionally provoke the gathered crowd into a violent riot. The police arrest him before the crowd reacts. What is his penalty?
answer: Because he wantonly did an illegal act intending to provoke a riot, but the offence of rioting was *not* committed, he is punishable under Section 192 with imprisonment up to six months, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 192, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot-if rioting be committed; if not
question: Under this section, does the provocative act itself have to be illegal, or can it be a perfectly legal but offensive act?
answer: The section specifically requires the person to give provocation "by doing anything which is illegal." A legal (even if highly offensive) act does not satisfy the elements. [Source: Section 192, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot-if rioting be committed; if not
question: If a comedian tells an offensive joke on stage (which is not illegal) and the audience gets so angry they start a riot, is the comedian guilty under Section 192?
answer: No, because telling the joke was not "doing anything which is illegal," the comedian cannot be charged under Section 192 for provoking the riot. [Source: Section 192, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot-if rioting be committed; if not
question: What is the consequence of penalizing the provocateur even if the riot does *not* happen?
answer: The consequence is that law enforcement can intervene and arrest agitators who are actively trying to spark mass violence (like throwing a rock at a peaceful protest) before their reckless actions successfully ignite a deadly riot. [Source: Section 192, BNS 2023]

section_title: Liability of owner, occupier, etc., of land on which an unlawful assembly or riot takes
question: If a riot happens on your property, can you be fined if you don't call the police?
answer: Yes, a landowner or occupier can be fined up to 1,000 rupees if they know a riot or unlawful assembly is happening on their land and fail to notify the police or try to stop it. [Source: Section 193, BNS 2023]

section_title: Liability of owner, occupier, etc., of land on which an unlawful assembly or riot takes
question: How does Section 193 of BNS 2023 impose duties on landowners regarding riots?
answer: Section 193 of BNS 2023 penalizes landowners, occupiers, and their agents with a fine (up to 1,000 rupees in some cases) if they fail to notify police, prevent, or suppress an unlawful assembly or riot taking place on their land or for their benefit. [Source: Section 193, BNS 2023]

section_title: Liability of owner, occupier, etc., of land on which an unlawful assembly or riot takes
question: A factory owner realizes his hired security guards have formed an unlawful assembly on his property to violently evict squatters. He sits back and watches without calling the police. Can he be penalized?
answer: Yes, under sub-section (2), because the riot was committed for his benefit on his land, and he failed to use lawful means to prevent or suppress it, he is punishable with a fine. [Source: Section 193, BNS 2023]

section_title: Liability of owner, occupier, etc., of land on which an unlawful assembly or riot takes
question: Under sub-section (1), what two specific actions must an owner/occupier take to avoid a fine when they know a riot is occurring on their land?
answer: They must 1) give the "earliest notice thereof in his or their power to the officer in charge at the nearest police station" and 2) "use all lawful means in his or their power to disperse or suppress the riot." [Source: Section 193, BNS 2023]

section_title: Liability of owner, occupier, etc., of land on which an unlawful assembly or riot takes
question: A landlord lives in another country and has no agent on site. A riot spontaneously breaks out on his vacant lot in India. He finds out a week later. Can he be fined?
answer: No, the statute requires the owner or their agent to act while "knowing that such offence is being or has been committed, or having reason to believe it is likely to be committed." Without real-time knowledge, he cannot be fined for failing to act. [Source: Section 193, BNS 2023]

section_title: Liability of owner, occupier, etc., of land on which an unlawful assembly or riot takes
question: What is the legal consequence of making landowners criminally liable for the riots of others on their property?
answer: The consequence is that it prevents feudal landlords, corporate owners, or community leaders from feigning ignorance while "looking the other way" as mobs commit violence on their behalf or on their territory. [Source: Section 193, BNS 2023]

section_title: Affray
question: What is the crime of "affray"?
answer: An affray is when two or more people disturb the public peace by fighting in a public place. [Source: Section 194, BNS 2023]

section_title: Affray
question: How does Section 194 of BNS 2023 define and punish an affray?
answer: Section 194 of BNS 2023 defines an affray as two or more persons fighting in a public place and disturbing the public peace, which is punishable by up to 1 month imprisonment, or a fine up to 1,000 rupees, or both. [Source: Section 194, BNS 2023]

section_title: Affray
question: Two men get into a loud, violent fistfight in the middle of a busy street market, causing bystanders to flee in panic. What specific offence have they committed?
answer: Because they are two persons fighting in a public place and disturbing the public peace, they have committed an "affray" under sub-section (1), punishable under sub-section (2). [Source: Section 194, BNS 2023]

section_title: Affray
question: What is the critical locational element required for a fight to be considered an "affray"?
answer: The fighting must take place "in a public place." [Source: Section 194, BNS 2023]

section_title: Affray
question: Two brothers get into a loud, violent fistfight inside their private, soundproofed basement. Are they guilty of an affray?
answer: No, because the fight did not occur "in a public place" and did not "disturb the public peace," it is not an affray (though it could be assault or battery). [Source: Section 194, BNS 2023]

section_title: Affray
question: What is the consequence of defining "affray" as a distinct crime from assault?
answer: The consequence is that the law protects the general public's right to peace and safety in shared spaces; police can arrest brawlers for terrorizing bystanders, even if neither fighter wants to press assault charges against the other. [Source: Section 194, BNS 2023]

section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc
question: What is the penalty for attacking a police officer who is trying to break up a riot?
answer: Assaulting, obstructing, or using criminal force against a public servant trying to disperse a riot is punishable by up to three years in prison, or a fine of at least 25,000 rupees, or both. [Source: Section 195, BNS 2023]

section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc
question: How does Section 195 of BNS 2023 penalize violence against public servants during a riot?
answer: Section 195 of BNS 2023 prescribes up to 3 years imprisonment, or a minimum fine of 25,000 rupees, or both, for assaulting or obstructing a public servant suppressing a riot; merely threatening or attempting to do so carries up to 1 year imprisonment or a fine. [Source: Section 195, BNS 2023]

section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc
question: A police officer is trying to disperse an unlawful assembly. A rioter throws a brick at the officer, hitting his shield. What is the penalty for the rioter under this specific section?
answer: Because the rioter used criminal force on a public servant endeavouring to disperse an unlawful assembly, he is punishable under sub-section (1) with imprisonment up to three years, or a minimum fine of 25,000 rupees, or both. [Source: Section 195, BNS 2023]

section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc
question: Under sub-section (1), what is the unique statutory requirement regarding the fine if the judge chooses to impose one?
answer: The fine "shall not be less than twenty-five thousand rupees." [Source: Section 195, BNS 2023]

section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc
question: A police officer is off-duty, out of uniform, and grocery shopping when he gets into a personal argument with a cashier. The cashier pushes him. Is the cashier guilty under Section 195?
answer: No, because the officer was not acting "in the discharge of his duty... in endeavouring to disperse an unlawful assembly, or to suppress a riot or affray," Section 195 does not apply. [Source: Section 195, BNS 2023]

section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc
question: What is the consequence of making the *threat* or *attempt* to obstruct an officer during a riot a separate, punishable offence (up to 1 year) under sub-section (2)?
answer: The consequence is that police can immediately arrest and neutralize aggressive individuals who are posturing, shouting threats, or trying to block their path, without having to wait for the individual to actually strike a blow. [Source: Section 195, BNS 2023]

section_title: Promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts prejudicial to maintenance of harmony
question: What is the penalty for spreading hatred between different religious or racial communities?
answer: Promoting enmity, hatred, or ill-will between different groups based on religion, race, language, or caste is punishable by up to three years in prison, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 196, BNS 2023]

section_title: Promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts prejudicial to maintenance of harmony
question: How does Section 196 of BNS 2023 punish the promotion of communal disharmony?
answer: Section 196 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 3 years imprisonment (or 5 years if committed in a place of worship) and a fine for promoting enmity between groups on grounds of religion, race, etc., or organizing training with criminal force against such groups. [Source: Section 196, BNS 2023]

section_title: Promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts prejudicial to maintenance of harmony
question: A politician gives a fiery speech urging their regional linguistic group to violently expel all people from a neighboring state who speak a different language, leading to public panic. What is the penalty?
answer: Because the politician by spoken words promoted feelings of enmity between different language and regional groups, they are punishable under sub-section (1)(a) with imprisonment up to three years, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 196, BNS 2023]

section_title: Promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts prejudicial to maintenance of harmony
question: Under sub-section (2), what specific locations trigger the enhanced five-year maximum sentence for promoting enmity?
answer: The enhanced sentence is triggered if the offence is committed "in any place of worship or in any assembly engaged in the performance of religious worship or religious ceremonies." [Source: Section 196, BNS 2023]

section_title: Promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts prejudicial to maintenance of harmony
question: If a sociologist publishes an academic, data-driven paper objectively detailing historical conflicts between two castes, without any attempt or intent to promote hatred, is it a crime?
answer: No, because the act must "promote or attempt to promote... disharmony or feelings of enmity, hatred or ill-will." Objective academic research lacking this intent or effect does not violate Section 196. [Source: Section 196, BNS 2023]

section_title: Promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts prejudicial to maintenance of harmony
question: What is the systemic consequence of sub-section (1)(c) penalizing the organization of "drills" or "training" against a specific community?
answer: The consequence is that the law can dismantle communal militias, extremist training camps, and paramilitary hate groups before they execute a coordinated attack, treating the very act of preparing violence against a community as a serious offence. [Source: Section 196, BNS 2023]

section_title: Imputations, assertions prejudicial to national integration
question: Is it a crime to publicly claim that people of a certain religion cannot be loyal citizens of India?
answer: Yes, publishing imputations that a specific religious, racial, or linguistic group cannot bear allegiance to the Constitution or uphold India's sovereignty is punishable by up to three years in prison and a fine. [Source: Section 197, BNS 2023]

section_title: Imputations, assertions prejudicial to national integration
question: How does Section 197 of BNS 2023 penalize speech that harms national integration?
answer: Section 197 of BNS 2023 prescribes up to 3 years imprisonment (or 5 years in a place of worship) for making assertions that a specific group cannot bear allegiance to India, should be denied citizenship rights, or by spreading false information jeopardizing India's sovereignty. [Source: Section 197, BNS 2023]

section_title: Imputations, assertions prejudicial to national integration
question: A radical blogger publishes an article stating that members of Caste X inherently hate India and should immediately be stripped of their voting rights. What offence has the blogger committed?
answer: Because the blogger asserted that a class of persons shall, by reason of their caste, be deprived of their rights as citizens of India, the blogger is punishable under sub-section (1)(b) with imprisonment up to three years. [Source: Section 197, BNS 2023]

section_title: Imputations, assertions prejudicial to national integration
question: Under clause (d) of sub-section (1), what must be the potential impact of the false or misleading information to qualify as an offence?
answer: The false or misleading information must be "jeopardising the sovereignty, unity and integrity or security of India." [Source: Section 197, BNS 2023]

section_title: Imputations, assertions prejudicial to national integration
question: A lawyer argues in court that a specific law (not a community) violates the Constitution and asks for it to be struck down. Does this violate Section 197?
answer: No, criticizing a law is not the same as making an imputation against a "class of persons" regarding their allegiance to India, so it does not fall under Section 197. [Source: Section 197, BNS 2023]

section_title: Imputations, assertions prejudicial to national integration
question: What is the consequence of elevating these specific types of hate speech to crimes against "national integration"?
answer: The consequence is that the State treats rhetoric that fundamentally questions the citizenship, loyalty, or basic rights of minority communities not just as personal defamation, but as a direct threat to the survival and unity of the secular republic itself. [Source: Section 197, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying law, with intent to cause injury to any person
question: What is the penalty if a government official intentionally ignores a legal rule just to hurt someone's case?
answer: A public servant who knowingly disobeys a direction of the law with the intent to cause injury to any person is punishable by up to one year of simple imprisonment, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 198, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying law, with intent to cause injury to any person
question: How does Section 198 of BNS 2023 punish malicious disobedience by public servants?
answer: Section 198 of BNS 2023 imposes simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or a fine, or both, on any public servant who knowingly disobeys a legal direction regarding their conduct, intending or knowing it will likely cause injury to a person. [Source: Section 198, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying law, with intent to cause injury to any person
question: A tax officer is legally directed to release a businessman's frozen bank account within 24 hours of a court order. The officer personally hates the businessman and intentionally delays it for a month, causing the business to default on loans. What is the officer's crime?
answer: Because the officer knowingly disobeyed a direction of law regarding his conduct, intending to cause injury to the businessman, he is punishable under Section 198 with simple imprisonment up to one year or a fine. [Source: Section 198, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying law, with intent to cause injury to any person
question: What specific type of imprisonment is prescribed under this section?
answer: The section exclusively prescribes "simple imprisonment" (up to one year). [Source: Section 198, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying law, with intent to cause injury to any person
question: A clerk misreads a newly updated legal circular and accidentally files a citizen's application in the wrong folder, delaying their pension by a month. Is the clerk guilty under Section 198?
answer: No, the disobedience was an honest mistake. The statute requires the public servant to act "knowingly" and with the intent or knowledge that it will "cause injury to any person." [Source: Section 198, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying law, with intent to cause injury to any person
question: What is the consequence of limiting the punishment for this offence to "simple imprisonment" of one year?
answer: The consequence is that while the law provides a remedy against malicious bureaucratic sabotage, it keeps the penalty relatively mild to prevent paralyzing government operations with the constant threat of severe, rigorous prison sentences for administrative disputes. [Source: Section 198, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying direction under law
question: What happens if a police officer refuses to record a victim's complaint about a serious crime like rape?
answer: A public servant who fails to record information related to specific serious offences (like rape or sexual assault) is punishable by rigorous imprisonment for between six months and two years, plus a fine. [Source: Section 199, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying direction under law
question: How does Section 199 of BNS 2023 penalize a public servant's failure to follow investigative directions?
answer: Section 199 of BNS 2023 prescribes rigorous imprisonment from 6 months up to 2 years, and a fine, for a public servant who knowingly disobeys laws regulating investigations, improperly requires a person's attendance, or fails to record complaints for specified serious offences (e.g., Section 64, 65). [Source: Section 199, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying direction under law
question: A police inspector refuses to write down an FIR from a woman reporting an acid attack (covered under Section 124), telling her to "go settle it privately." What is the mandatory minimum penalty for the inspector?
answer: Because he failed to record information relating to a cognizable offence punishable under Section 124, he is punishable under clause (c) with rigorous imprisonment for a term "which shall not be less than six months." [Source: Section 199, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying direction under law
question: Under clause (a), what specific illegal demand made by an investigating officer is criminalized?
answer: It criminalizes knowingly disobeying any direction of law "which prohibits him from requiring the attendance at any place of any person for the purpose of investigation." [Source: Section 199, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying direction under law
question: If a police officer fails to record a complaint about a stolen bicycle (a minor theft), does it trigger the strict penalties of clause (c) in Section 199?
answer: No, clause (c) specifically lists the serious offences (like Sections 64, 65, 70, 124, etc., primarily relating to sexual offences and grievous harm) for which failing to record information is a crime under this section. [Source: Section 199, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant disobeying direction under law
question: What is the systemic consequence of establishing a mandatory minimum of 6 months rigorous imprisonment for police officers who refuse to record rape cases?
answer: The consequence is that it combats the entrenched systemic issue of "burking" (police refusing to register serious crimes against vulnerable victims), ensuring victims of sexual violence have legal recourse against officers who deny them access to the justice system. [Source: Section 199, BNS 2023]

section_title: Punishment for non-treatment of victim
question: What is the penalty if a hospital turns away a rape or acid attack victim and refuses to give them free first aid?
answer: A person in charge of a hospital who fails to provide free, immediate medical treatment to victims of certain serious offences, as required by law, is punishable by up to one year in prison, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 200, BNS 2023]

section_title: Punishment for non-treatment of victim
question: How does Section 200 of BNS 2023 punish the denial of medical treatment to victims?
answer: Section 200 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 1 year imprisonment, or a fine, or both, on anyone in charge of a public or private hospital who contravenes Section 397 of the BNSS, 2023 (which mandates free immediate treatment for victims of specific crimes). [Source: Section 200, BNS 2023]

section_title: Punishment for non-treatment of victim
question: The head administrator of a private luxury hospital orders staff to refuse entry to an acid attack victim because she has no health insurance, violating Section 397 of the BNSS. What is the administrator's penalty?
answer: Because he is in charge of a private hospital and contravened the provisions of Section 397 of the BNSS, 2023, he is punishable under Section 200 with imprisonment up to one year, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 200, BNS 2023]

section_title: Punishment for non-treatment of victim
question: Does this section only apply to government-run public hospitals?
answer: No, the statute explicitly applies to anyone in charge of a hospital, "public or private, whether run by the Central Government, the State Government, local bodies or any other person." [Source: Section 200, BNS 2023]

section_title: Punishment for non-treatment of victim
question: A victim is brought to a small community clinic, but the clinic lacks the surgical equipment needed to treat the massive trauma, so the doctor stabilizes the victim and immediately transfers them to a major hospital. Is the doctor guilty of "non-treatment"?
answer: No, providing stabilizing first aid and an immediate medical transfer out of sheer necessity (because the facility lacks capacity) is medically appropriate and does not constitute a malicious contravention of the legal duty to treat. [Source: Section 200, BNS 2023]

section_title: Punishment for non-treatment of victim
question: What is the legal consequence of making hospital administrators criminally liable for refusing to treat victims of severe crimes?
answer: The consequence is that it legally prioritizes the right to life and bodily integrity over hospital profit margins or administrative bureaucracy, ensuring that victims of extreme violence receive immediate, life-saving care regardless of their financial status. [Source: Section 200, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant framing an incorrect document with intent to cause injury
question: What is the penalty for a public servant who intentionally falsifies an official record to harm someone?
answer: A public servant who prepares or translates a document incorrectly, intending to cause injury to any person, is punishable by up to three years in prison, or a fine, or both. [Source: Section 201, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant framing an incorrect document with intent to cause injury
question: How does Section 201 of BNS 2023 penalize the falsification of official documents by public servants?
answer: Section 201 of BNS 2023 prescribes up to 3 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both, for any public servant who frames, prepares, or translates a document/electronic record incorrectly, intending or knowing it will likely cause injury to someone. [Source: Section 201, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant framing an incorrect document with intent to cause injury
question: A court clerk is ordered to translate a witness statement. The clerk personally dislikes the defendant, so he intentionally alters the translation to make the defendant look guilty. What is the clerk's crime?
answer: Because he is a public servant charged with translation and framed an incorrect document intending to cause injury to the defendant, he is punishable under Section 201 with imprisonment up to three years. [Source: Section 201, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant framing an incorrect document with intent to cause injury
question: Does this section apply to a public servant who alters a private document that they found on the street?
answer: No, the statute explicitly requires the public servant to be "charged with the preparation or translation of any document or electronic record" in their capacity "as such public servant." [Source: Section 201, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant framing an incorrect document with intent to cause injury
question: A government surveyor makes a massive mathematical error on a property map, causing a citizen to lose half their land. Is the surveyor guilty under Section 201?
answer: No, because the surveyor did not frame the document in a manner "which he knows or believes to be incorrect." Honest mistakes, even devastating ones, lack the requisite criminal intent. [Source: Section 201, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant framing an incorrect document with intent to cause injury
question: What is the consequence of establishing a specific offence for public servants falsifying documents (up to 3 years) instead of just using general forgery laws?
answer: The consequence is that it addresses the unique breach of public trust when an official misuses their state-granted authority to create "official" falsehoods, ensuring strict accountability for the integrity of government records. [Source: Section 201, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully engaging in trade
question: Can a government employee run a side business if their contract forbids it?
answer: No, a public servant who engages in trade while legally bound not to do so is punishable by up to one year of simple imprisonment, a fine, both, or community service. [Source: Section 202, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully engaging in trade
question: How does Section 202 of BNS 2023 punish public servants for engaging in forbidden commercial activities?
answer: Section 202 of BNS 2023 imposes simple imprisonment up to 1 year, a fine, both, or community service on any public servant who engages in trade when legally bound not to do so. [Source: Section 202, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully engaging in trade
question: An active-duty police inspector, who is strictly forbidden by law from commercial enterprise, secretly opens a restaurant in his wife's name but personally manages the finances and operations. What offence has he committed?
answer: Because he is a public servant legally bound not to engage in trade and engaged in trade, he is punishable under Section 202 with simple imprisonment up to one year, a fine, or community service. [Source: Section 202, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully engaging in trade
question: What non-custodial alternative sentence is explicitly listed in this section alongside simple imprisonment and fines?
answer: The section explicitly lists "community service" as an alternative punishment. [Source: Section 202, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully engaging in trade
question: A low-level municipal sweeper sells homemade crafts on the weekend. The municipal bylaws have no rule preventing sweeping staff from having outside businesses. Is the sweeper guilty?
answer: No, because the sweeper is not "legally bound as such public servant not to engage in trade," their side business does not violate Section 202. [Source: Section 202, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully engaging in trade
question: What is the systemic consequence of criminalizing unauthorized trading by public servants?
answer: The consequence is that it prevents conflicts of interest, corruption, and the misuse of official power to gain an unfair advantage in private business, ensuring officials remain fully dedicated to public service. [Source: Section 202, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property
question: What happens if a government auctioneer secretly bids on the seized property they are supposed to be selling?
answer: A public servant who unlawfully purchases or bids for property they are legally barred from buying is punishable by up to two years of simple imprisonment, a fine, or both, and the property will be confiscated. [Source: Section 203, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property
question: How does Section 203 of BNS 2023 penalize public servants for illicit property purchases?
answer: Section 203 of BNS 2023 prescribes simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or a fine, or both, and confiscation of the purchased property, for a public servant who purchases or bids on property they are legally bound not to buy. [Source: Section 203, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property
question: A tax recovery officer organizes the auction of a defaulter's seized mansion. The officer gives his brother cash to secretly bid on and buy the mansion on the officer's behalf. What is the penalty?
answer: Because the officer purchased property he was legally bound not to purchase, in the name of another, he is punishable under Section 203 with imprisonment up to two years, and the mansion shall be confiscated. [Source: Section 203, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property
question: What specific legal mechanism does the statute include to prevent the public servant from keeping the illicitly acquired asset?
answer: The statute mandates that "the property, if purchased, shall be confiscated." [Source: Section 203, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property
question: A police officer buys a used car from a public dealership on his day off. Is this a crime under Section 203?
answer: No, unless the officer was "legally bound as such public servant, not to purchase or bid for [that] certain property" (e.g., if it was a seized vehicle he was investigating). General purchases are not criminalized. [Source: Section 203, BNS 2023]

section_title: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property
question: What is the legal consequence of expanding this offence to include bidding "in the name of another, or jointly, or in shares with others"?
answer: The consequence is that it closes loopholes where corrupt officials use proxy buyers, shell companies, or family members to illegally acquire seized or state property at artificially depressed prices. [Source: Section 203, BNS 2023]

section_title: Personating a public servant
question: What is the penalty for pretending to be a police officer or government official?
answer: Falsely pretending to hold a public office, and attempting to do any act under the color of that office, is punishable by imprisonment between six months and three years, plus a fine. [Source: Section 204, BNS 2023]

section_title: Personating a public servant
question: How does Section 204 of BNS 2023 punish the impersonation of public servants?
answer: Section 204 of BNS 2023 imposes a mandatory minimum of 6 months, and up to 3 years imprisonment, plus a fine, on anyone who falsely personates a public servant and does or attempts to do an act under the colour of such office. [Source: Section 204, BNS 2023]

section_title: Personating a public servant
question: A con artist buys a fake ID badge, claims to be a health inspector, and enters a restaurant demanding a 5,000 rupee "fine" for a fake health code violation. What is the minimum sentence?
answer: Because he personated a public servant and attempted an act under the colour of that office, he is punishable under Section 204 with imprisonment "which shall not be less than six months." [Source: Section 204, BNS 2023]

section_title: Personating a public servant
question: Under Section 204, is it enough just to tell someone you are a public servant to commit the offence?
answer: No, the statute requires that the person not only pretends to hold the office, but also "in such assumed character does or attempts to do any act under colour of such office." [Source: Section 204, BNS 2023]

section_title: Personating a public servant
question: An actor plays the role of a judge in a stage play, wearing a robe and banging a gavel in front of an audience. Is the actor guilty of impersonating a public servant?
answer: No, because the actor is not doing an act "under colour of such office" with the intent or effect of acting as a real public servant; it is clearly theatrical and not a false pretense of holding actual office. [Source: Section 204, BNS 2023]

section_title: Personating a public servant
question: What is the consequence of instituting a strict mandatory minimum sentence of 6 months for impersonating a public servant?
answer: The consequence is that the State severely deters fraudsters who undermine public trust in government authority by using fake official status to extort, intimidate, or scam ordinary citizens. [Source: Section 204, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by public servant with fraudulent intent
question: What is the penalty for wearing a fake police uniform just to trick people into thinking you are a cop?
answer: Wearing the uniform or carrying the token of a public servant, intending to make people believe you belong to that class of servants, is punishable by up to three months in prison, or a fine up to 5,000 rupees. [Source: Section 205, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by public servant with fraudulent intent
question: How does Section 205 of BNS 2023 regulate the unauthorized use of official uniforms?
answer: Section 205 of BNS 2023 prescribes up to 3 months imprisonment, or a fine up to 5,000 rupees, for anyone who wears the garb or carries the token of a public servant class they don't belong to, intending or knowing it will deceive people. [Source: Section 205, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by public servant with fraudulent intent
question: A teenager buys a realistic-looking traffic police uniform and stands at an intersection, doing nothing but enjoying the fact that drivers are slowing down and saluting him. Can he be arrested?
answer: Yes, because he wore garb resembling a public servant's uniform with the intention that it be believed he belongs to that class, he is punishable under Section 205 with imprisonment up to three months or a fine. [Source: Section 205, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by public servant with fraudulent intent
question: How does the *actus reus* of Section 205 (Wearing garb) differ from Section 204 (Personating)?
answer: Section 205 only requires the passive act of *wearing* the uniform or carrying the token with intent to deceive, whereas Section 204 requires actively *doing or attempting to do an act* under the color of the office. [Source: Section 205, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by public servant with fraudulent intent
question: If a person attends a private Halloween costume party dressed as a generic postal worker, with no intent for anyone to believe they are a real on-duty mail carrier, is it an offence?
answer: No, the statute requires the "intention that it may be believed, or with the knowledge that it is likely to be believed, that he belongs to that class of public servants." A costume party lacks this deceptive intent. [Source: Section 205, BNS 2023]

section_title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by public servant with fraudulent intent
question: What is the legal consequence of criminalizing the mere wearing of an official uniform (Section 205) even if the person doesn't extort anyone?
answer: The consequence is that law enforcement can preemptively arrest impersonators who are loitering or "patrolling" before they actually commit a substantive fraud or extortion (which would trigger the harsher Section 204). [Source: Section 205, BNS 2023]

section_title: Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding
question: What is the penalty for going into hiding to avoid being served with a legal summons?
answer: Absconding to avoid a summons from a public servant is punishable by up to one month in prison or a 5,000 rupee fine; if the summons is for a court appearance, it increases to six months or a 10,000 rupee fine. [Source: Section 206, BNS 2023]

section_title: Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding
question: How does Section 206 of BNS 2023 punish absconding to evade legal service?
answer: Section 206 of BNS 2023 prescribes simple imprisonment up to 1 month or a 5,000 rupee fine for absconding to avoid general summons, escalating to 6 months imprisonment or a 10,000 rupee fine if the summons requires attending Court. [Source: Section 206, BNS 2023]

section_title: Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding
question: A businessman knows he is being sued. When he sees the court process server pull into his driveway with a court summons to produce financial documents, he jumps out the back window and hides in the woods. What is the maximum penalty?
answer: Because he absconded to avoid a summons to produce a document in a Court, he is punishable under clause (b) with simple imprisonment up to six months, or a fine up to ten thousand rupees, or both. [Source: Section 206, BNS 2023]

section_title: Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding
question: Under clause (b), what specific destination of the summons triggers the harsher 6-month penalty?
answer: The harsher penalty applies if the summons or order is "to attend in person or by agent, or to produce a document or an electronic record in a Court." [Source: Section 206, BNS 2023]

section_title: Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding
question: A man goes on a month-long backpacking trip without internet. During that time, a public servant tries to serve him a notice. He wasn't hiding; he just wasn't home. Is he guilty under Section 206?
answer: No, the statute requires that the person absconds "in order to avoid being served." A coincidental absence lacks the specific intent to evade service. [Source: Section 206, BNS 2023]

section_title: Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding
question: What is the consequence of having two different tiers of punishment in this section?
answer: The consequence is that while avoiding administrative notices from generic public servants is a minor offence, evading the formal judicial process (a Court) is treated as a significantly more serious contempt of lawful authority. [Source: Section 206, BNS 2023]

section_title: Preventing service of summons or other proceeding, or preventing publication thereof
question: Can you be punished for tearing down a legal notice that an official taped to your door?
answer: Yes, intentionally preventing the service or lawful affixing of a legal notice, or removing it once affixed, is punishable by up to one month in prison, or six months if it relates to a court proceeding. [Source: Section 207, BNS 2023]

section_title: Preventing service of summons or other proceeding, or preventing publication thereof
question: How does Section 207 of BNS 2023 penalize interference with the service of legal documents?
answer: Section 207 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 1 month imprisonment or a 5,000 rupee fine for intentionally preventing service, affixing, or proclamation of a notice, increasing to 6 months or 10,000 rupees if it pertains to a Court appearance or document production. [Source: Section 207, BNS 2023]

section_title: Preventing service of summons or other proceeding, or preventing publication thereof
question: A process server legally staples a court summons to a suspect's front door requiring him to attend a hearing. The suspect's roommate immediately rips it off and throws it away so the suspect won't see it. What is the roommate's crime?
answer: Because the roommate intentionally removed a summons to attend a Court from a place to which it was lawfully affixed, he is punishable under clause (b) with simple imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to ten thousand rupees. [Source: Section 207, BNS 2023]

section_title: Preventing service of summons or other proceeding, or preventing publication thereof
question: What specific method of public announcement, aside from physical documents, is protected from interference under this section?
answer: The section specifically protects the "lawful making of any proclamation" from being intentionally prevented. [Source: Section 207, BNS 2023]

section_title: Preventing service of summons or other proceeding, or preventing publication thereof
question: A heavy rainstorm washes away a loosely taped public notice from a bulletin board. The property owner sweeps it into the trash. Is the owner guilty under Section 207?
answer: No, the owner did not intentionally remove it from the place to which it was "lawfully affixed" to prevent its publication; nature removed it, and the owner just cleaned up the litter. [Source: Section 207, BNS 2023]

section_title: Preventing service of summons or other proceeding, or preventing publication thereof
question: What is the systemic consequence of criminalizing the prevention of service?
answer: The consequence is that it guarantees the integrity of procedural due process; defendants cannot delay justice indefinitely by physically blocking process servers, tearing up paperwork, or shouting down public criers. [Source: Section 207, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from public servant
question: What happens if you just ignore a subpoena and don't show up to a meeting ordered by a public servant?
answer: Intentionally omitting to attend at the specified time and place, or leaving early without permission, is punishable by up to one month in prison, or six months if the order was to appear in Court. [Source: Section 208, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from public servant
question: How does Section 208 of BNS 2023 penalize the failure to attend legally mandated proceedings?
answer: Section 208 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 1 month imprisonment or a 5,000 rupee fine for intentionally failing to attend a meeting ordered by a public servant, escalating to 6 months or 10,000 rupees if the order was to attend a Court. [Source: Section 208, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from public servant
question: A witness is legally summoned to testify before a District Judge at 10:00 AM. He arrives, but gets bored and leaves the courthouse at 10:15 AM before the judge calls him. What offence has he committed?
answer: Because he departed from the place where he was bound to attend before the time it was lawful for him to depart, in obedience to a Court summons, he is punishable under clause (b) with simple imprisonment up to six months. [Source: Section 208, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from public servant
question: What is the necessary *mens rea* (mental state) for a person to be guilty of non-attendance under this section?
answer: The person must "intentionally" omit to attend or depart early. Accidental non-attendance (e.g., getting in a car crash) is not an offence. [Source: Section 208, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from public servant
question: A citizen is summoned to the municipal office by a clerk who actually has no legal authority to issue such summons. The citizen ignores it. Is the citizen guilty?
answer: No, the statute requires the summons to proceed "from any public servant legally competent, as such public servant, to issue the same." If the clerk lacked competence, ignoring the void summons is not a crime. [Source: Section 208, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from public servant
question: What is the consequence of explicitly criminalizing early departure alongside non-attendance?
answer: The consequence is that individuals cannot exploit a loophole by technically showing up for one minute and then fleeing; they remain under the lawful authority of the public servant until officially dismissed. [Source: Section 208, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-appearance in response to a proclamation under section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik
question: What is the penalty for ignoring a formal proclamation demanding you surrender to authorities?
answer: Failing to appear in response to a Section 84 proclamation is punishable by up to three years in prison; if the person is formally declared a "proclaimed offender," the penalty jumps to seven years. [Source: Section 209, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-appearance in response to a proclamation under section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik
question: How does Section 209 of BNS 2023 punish non-appearance by a proclaimed individual?
answer: Section 209 of BNS 2023 imposes up to 3 years imprisonment (or community service) for failing to appear in response to a BNSS Section 84 proclamation, and up to 7 years imprisonment plus a fine if formally declared a proclaimed offender. [Source: Section 209, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-appearance in response to a proclamation under section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik
question: A murder suspect flees the state. The court issues a proclamation under BNSS Section 84 requiring him to appear, and then makes a declaration pronouncing him a "proclaimed offender." He still refuses to appear. What is the maximum prison sentence for this specific non-appearance?
answer: Because a declaration was made pronouncing him a proclaimed offender, he is punishable under Section 209 with imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, plus a fine. [Source: Section 209, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-appearance in response to a proclamation under section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik
question: Under this section, what non-custodial alternative sentence is available for a person who fails to appear *before* they are officially declared a "proclaimed offender"?
answer: The statute lists "community service" as a potential alternative punishment for the baseline 3-year offence (but not for the enhanced 7-year offence). [Source: Section 209, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-appearance in response to a proclamation under section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik
question: If the court's original proclamation failed to specify a "place" and a "time" for the person to appear, and the person doesn't show up, can they be convicted under Section 209?
answer: No, the statute strictly requires failure to appear "at the specified place and the specified time." A procedurally defective proclamation that lacks these details cannot trigger this criminal penalty. [Source: Section 209, BNS 2023]

section_title: Non-appearance in response to a proclamation under section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik
question: What is the consequence of treating the evasion of a Section 84 proclamation as a standalone crime with up to 7 years imprisonment?
answer: The consequence is that it heavily incentivizes fugitives to surrender, as remaining on the run transforms from mere evasion into a severe independent felony, adding massive prison time even if they beat the underlying charges. [Source: Section 209, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to produce document or electronic record to public servant by person legally
question: What happens if a person intentionally refuses to hand over a document when legally ordered by a public servant?
answer: Refusing to produce a requested document is punishable by up to one month in prison, or up to six months if the document was ordered to be produced in a Court. [Source: Section 210, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to produce document or electronic record to public servant by person legally
question: How does Section 210 of BNS 2023 penalize the withholding of documents from authorities?
answer: Section 210 of BNS 2023 prescribes up to 1 month simple imprisonment or a 5,000 rupee fine for intentionally omitting to produce a document to a public servant, escalating to 6 months or 10,000 rupees if the document must be produced to a Court. [Source: Section 210, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to produce document or electronic record to public servant by person legally
question: A bank manager is legally ordered by a District Court to produce a suspect's electronic transaction records. The manager intentionally ignores the order to protect the bank's wealthy client. What offence has the manager committed?
answer: Because he intentionally omitted to produce an electronic record to a Court when legally bound to do so, he is punishable under clause (b) with simple imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to ten thousand rupees. [Source: Section 210, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to produce document or electronic record to public servant by person legally
question: What modern form of record-keeping is explicitly included alongside traditional "documents" in this section?
answer: The section explicitly includes an "electronic record." [Source: Section 210, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to produce document or electronic record to public servant by person legally
question: A citizen is ordered to produce a 20-year-old tax receipt. They sincerely search their entire house but realize the document was destroyed in a flood years ago. Are they guilty of an omission?
answer: No, the statute requires the person to "intentionally" omit to produce the document. Inability to produce a destroyed document is not an intentional omission. [Source: Section 210, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to produce document or electronic record to public servant by person legally
question: What is the legal consequence of differentiating between public servants and Courts in this section?
answer: The consequence is that while failing to provide paperwork to an administrative agency is a minor infraction, obstructing a formal judicial proceeding by hiding evidence is treated as a more severe offence requiring harsher penalties. [Source: Section 210, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to give notice or information to public servant by person legally bound to give
question: What is the penalty if you are legally required to report something to the police but you intentionally stay silent?
answer: Intentionally failing to give required information to a public servant is punishable by up to one month in prison; if the information was about a crime, the penalty increases to six months. [Source: Section 211, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to give notice or information to public servant by person legally bound to give
question: How does Section 211 of BNS 2023 penalize the omission to furnish legally required information?
answer: Section 211 of BNS 2023 imposes simple imprisonment up to 1 month or a 5,000 rupee fine for intentionally omitting to give legally required information, escalating to 6 months or 10,000 rupees if the information respects the commission of an offence or is required to apprehend an offender. [Source: Section 211, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to give notice or information to public servant by person legally bound to give
question: A doctor treats a patient for a gunshot wound. By law, the doctor is bound to report gunshot wounds to the police immediately. The doctor intentionally omits to do so to protect the patient. What is the doctor's penalty?
answer: Because the information required to be given respects the commission of an offence, the doctor is punishable under clause (b) with simple imprisonment up to six months, or a fine up to ten thousand rupees, or both. [Source: Section 211, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to give notice or information to public servant by person legally bound to give
question: Under clause (c), what specific section of the BNSS triggers a specialized 6-month penalty for failing to provide information?
answer: The penalty under clause (c) is triggered if the information is required by an order passed under "section 394 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023." [Source: Section 211, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to give notice or information to public servant by person legally bound to give
question: A citizen witnesses a petty theft but doesn't report it. Are they guilty under Section 211?
answer: It depends on whether they were "legally bound" to give notice. If the law does not specifically bind a random civilian to report that specific minor crime, the omission is not an offence under Section 211. [Source: Section 211, BNS 2023]

section_title: Omission to give notice or information to public servant by person legally bound to give
question: What is the consequence of escalating the punishment in clause (b) for omissions relating to offences?
answer: The consequence is that it severely penalizes covering up crimes; while ignoring administrative reporting requirements is a minor infraction, actively withholding evidence of a crime obstructs justice and allows offenders to escape. [Source: Section 211, BNS 2023]

section_title: Furnishing false information
question: What happens if you are legally required to report a crime, but you lie to the police about what happened?
answer: Furnishing false information to a public servant when legally bound to tell the truth is punishable by up to six months in prison; if the information relates to a crime, the penalty is up to two years. [Source: Section 212, BNS 2023]

section_title: Furnishing false information
question: How does Section 212 of BNS 2023 penalize furnishing false information to public servants?
answer: Section 212 of BNS 2023 prescribes simple imprisonment up to 6 months for furnishing false information when legally bound to provide it, which increases to 2 years imprisonment of either description if the false information pertains to the commission of an offence or apprehending an offender. [Source: Section 212, BNS 2023]

section_title: Furnishing false information
question: A village watchman knows a gang of robbers passed through his village heading North. When the police ask, the watchman (who is legally bound to report this) lies and says they went South. What is his crime?
answer: Because he furnished false information respecting the commission of an offence or apprehension of offenders, he is punishable under clause (b) with imprisonment of either description up to two years. [Source: Section 212, BNS 2023]

section_title: Furnishing false information
question: According to the Explanation in this section, does the word "offence" include crimes committed outside of India?
answer: Yes, the Explanation states that "offence" includes acts committed outside India which, if committed in India, would be punishable under specific listed sections (like murder, dacoity, etc.). [Source: Section 212, BNS 2023]

section_title: Furnishing false information
question: A witness to a hit-and-run is legally bound to describe the car. In the dark, they mistakenly thought it was a blue sedan, when it was actually a black SUV. They tell police it was a blue sedan. Are they guilty of furnishing false information?
answer: No, because they did not furnish information "which he knows or has reason to believe to be false." An honest mistake in perception lacks the requisite criminal knowledge. [Source: Section 212, BNS 2023]

section_title: Furnishing false information
question: What is the legal consequence of distinguishing "omission to give information" (Section 211) from "furnishing false information" (Section 212)?
answer: The consequence is that actively lying to mislead authorities (Section 212, up to 2 years) is treated as a much more severe obstruction of justice than merely staying silent (Section 211, up to 6 months). [Source: Section 212, BNS 2023]

section_title: Refusing oath or affirmation when duly required by public servant to make it
question: Is it a crime to refuse to swear an oath when a judge or official asks you to?
answer: Yes, refusing to bind yourself by an oath or affirmation to tell the truth, when legally required by a competent public servant, is punishable by up to six months in prison or a fine. [Source: Section 213, BNS 2023]

section_title: Refusing oath or affirmation when duly required by public servant to make it
question: How does Section 213 of BNS 2023 penalize the refusal to take an oath?
answer: Section 213 of BNS 2023 imposes simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or a fine up to 5,000 rupees, or both, on anyone who refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation when required by a legally competent public servant. [Source: Section 213, BNS 2023]

section_title: Refusing oath or affirmation when duly required by public servant to make it
question: A witness takes the stand in a civil trial. Before testifying, the judge asks him to swear an oath or make an affirmation to tell the truth. The witness flatly refuses, stating he doesn't believe in oaths. What is the penalty?
answer: Because he refused to bind himself by an oath or affirmation when required by a legally competent public servant (the judge), he is punishable under Section 213 with simple imprisonment up to six months or a fine. [Source: Section 213, BNS 2023]

section_title: Refusing oath or affirmation when duly required by public servant to make it
question: Does this section only apply to religious "oaths"?
answer: No, the statute explicitly includes refusing to bind oneself by an oath "or affirmation," accommodating individuals who object to religious oaths. [Source: Section 213, BNS 2023]

section_title: Refusing oath or affirmation when duly required by public servant to make it
